assets.direction_note: Higher scores reflect mandatory disclosure of legislators' earnings/assets, enhancing transparency.

censor.direction_note: More restrictive constitutional bans on censorship increase the transparency score.

govmed.direction_note: Prohibiting state ownership of media improves transparency, while mandatory state operation harms it.

infoacc.direction_note: A constitutional right of access to government information is the strongest indicator of transparency.

infoaccw.direction_note: Broader scopes of accessible documents (both personal and public) receive higher scores.

medcom.direction_note: Constitutional establishment of an independent media regulator supports transparency.

press.direction_note: Explicit constitutional freedom of the press enhances transparency.

prisonrg.direction_note: Public prison registries increase transparency about state detention practices.

pubmeet.direction_note: Mandating public legislative sessions improves transparency.

pubmin.direction_note: Publication of legislative deliberations signals higher transparency.

pubtri.direction_note: Constitutional requirement of public trials is a strong transparency guarantee.

radio.direction_note: Mention of radio indicates some constitutional attention to media transparency.

recvote.direction_note: Public recording of legislative votes enhances transparency; secret votes reduce it.

telecom.direction_note: Constitutional reference to telecommunications reflects minimal transparency relevance.

tv.direction_note: Constitutional mention of television provides limited insight into transparency.

medmark_1.direction_note: Prohibiting media monopolies or oligopolies supports a transparent media market.

medmark_2.direction_note: Constitutional endorsement of competition in the media market improves transparency.

medmark_3.direction_note: Affirming pluralism in media contributes positively to transparency.

medmark_4.direction_note: A balanced media market principle is mildly favorable for transparency.

medmark_5.direction_note: Stating fairness in media operations modestly enhances transparency.

medmark_6.direction_note: Allowing only a monopoly in media is detrimental to transparency.

medmark_7.direction_note: Permitting an oligopoly in media reduces transparency compared with competitive models.

em.direction_note: Presence of emergency provisions is seen as a necessary framework, scored higher than absence.

emdecl.direction_note: More diffuse or legislative involvement in declaring emergencies is scored higher.

emrights.direction_note: Ability to suspend rights lowers the score; a prohibition raises it.

inalrght.direction_note: Inalienable rights are fully protective, non‑inalienable rights are not.

terror.direction_note: Special terrorism provisions are generally less rights‑protective.

conpow_1.direction_note: Selection indicates the court can supervise elections, a modest democratic check.

conpow_2.direction_note: Court power to impeach the executive strengthens checks on emergency abuse.

conpow_3.direction_note: Court power to counter corruption modestly improves accountability.

conpow_4.direction_note: Court authority over party constitutionality adds a layer of oversight.

conpow_5.direction_note: Judicial review of states of emergency is a core constraint on executive power.

conpow_6.direction_note: Review of treaties is only tangentially related to emergency constraints.

emappr_1.direction_note: Selection means no approval is required, which is undemocratic.

emappr_2.direction_note: Head of State approval is a modest check; selection raises the score.

emappr_3.direction_note: Head of Government approval provides a democratic check; selection raises the score.

emappr_4.direction_note: Cabinet approval adds collective executive oversight; selection raises the score.

emappr_5.direction_note: Legislative (first/only chamber) approval is a strong democratic constraint; selection raises the score.

emappr_6.direction_note: Second chamber approval adds bicameral oversight; selection raises the score.

emappr_7.direction_note: Requiring both chambers is the strongest legislative check; selection raises the score.

emappr_8.direction_note: Constitutional Council approval adds a judicial check; selection raises the score.

emcond_1.direction_note: Allowing war/aggression as a trigger expands executive discretion; selection lowers the score.

emcond_2.direction_note: Internal security as a trigger broadens emergency scope; selection lowers the score.

emcond_3.direction_note: National disaster as a trigger is less restrictive; selection lowers the score.

emcond_4.direction_note: General danger as a trigger is a broad ground; selection lowers the score.

emcond_5.direction_note: Economic emergency as a trigger expands executive power; selection lowers the score.

emcond_6.direction_note: Threat to constitutional system as a trigger can be abused; selection lowers the score.

emother_1.direction_note: Prohibiting dissolution or term extension of the legislature protects democratic continuity; selection raises the score.

emother_2.direction_note: Bar on constitutional amendment during emergency safeguards the constitution; selection raises the score.

emother_3.direction_note: Allowing "all necessary measures" expands executive discretion; selection lowers the score.

emother_4.direction_note: Head of State issuing emergency decrees concentrates power; selection lowers the score.

emother_5.direction_note: Open‑ended executive authority during emergency is undemocratic; selection lowers the score.

comchief.direction_note: Higher scores when the commander‑in‑chief is a civilian (head of state or head of government).

military.direction_note: Constitutional mention of the armed forces enables civilian oversight; absence is scored lower.

milserv.direction_note: A constitutional duty of military service (conscription) is less democratic than no such duty.

nomil.direction_note: Explicit constitutional right to exemption (conscientious objection) is most democratic.

warap.direction_note: More civilian or legislative involvement in approving war declarations yields higher scores; military‑only or no‑approval mechanisms are lowest.

judcrts_1.direction_note: Presence of administrative courts is neutral for civilian control; both options receive a middle score.

judcrts_2.direction_note: Presence of a constitutional court is neutral for civilian control; both options receive a middle score.

judcrts_3.direction_note: Presence of amparo courts is neutral for civilian control; both options receive a middle score.

judcrts_4.direction_note: Existence of military courts undermines civilian control; their absence scores highest.

judcrts_5.direction_note: Special courts for public officials are neutral for civilian control; both options receive a middle score.

judcrts_6.direction_note: Tax courts are neutral for civilian control; both options receive a middle score.

judcrts_7.direction_note: Labor courts are neutral for civilian control; both options receive a middle score.

judcrts_8.direction_note: Religious courts are neutral for civilian control; both options receive a middle score.

judcrts_9.direction_note: Special courts (other) are neutral for civilian control; both options receive a middle score.

war_1.direction_note: Having the head of state able to declare war is democratic; lack of this power scores lower.

war_2.direction_note: Having the head of government able to declare war is democratic; lack of this power scores lower.

war_3.direction_note: Cabinet power to declare war is democratic; absence scores lower.

war_4.direction_note: Legislative (first/only chamber) authority to declare war is democratic; absence scores lower.

war_5.direction_note: Legislative (second chamber) authority to declare war is democratic; absence scores lower.

war_6.direction_note: Either chamber’s authority to declare war is democratic; absence scores lower.

war_7.direction_note: Joint bicameral approval is the most democratic war‑declaration mechanism; absence scores lower.

war_8.direction_note: National security council power to declare war is less democratic; its presence scores lower.

atgen.direction_note: More comprehensive constitutional provision of an attorney general (covering both criminal and civil matters) yields higher accountability.

bank.direction_note: Presence of a constitutionally mandated central bank modestly contributes to institutional accountability.

bankgoal.direction_note: Narrow, price‑stability‑only goals are scored higher as they limit political interference with monetary policy.

cabcoll.direction_note: Collective cabinet responsibility is scored highest; mixed or individual responsibility receives lower scores.

cc.direction_note: Constitutional establishment of a counter‑corruption commission signals strong accountability.

civil.direction_note: Merit‑based, non‑discriminatory civil‑service recruitment enhances accountability.

exinst.direction_note: Any additional independent regulatory agency improves accountability.

exinst1.direction_note: Open‑ended responses are not scored; only the 'not applicable' code is given a neutral value.

hr.direction_note: A constitutional human‑rights commission is a strong accountability mechanism.

intexec.direction_note: Greater legislative power to summon or require executive reporting increases accountability.

invexe.direction_note: Legislative investigative power over the executive is a key accountability tool.

jc.direction_note: A judicial council/commission for selection of judges strengthens judicial accountability.

oath.direction_note: Mandating an oath to the constitution reflects higher accountability of office‑holders.

ombuds.direction_note: A constitutional ombudsman is a strong mechanism for holding the government to account.

oversght.direction_note: Independent electoral oversight bodies (commission, court, or both) enhance accountability of the political process.

prevlead.direction_note: Punishing crimes of the previous regime scores higher; pardoning scores lowest.

profleg.direction_note: Stricter incompatibility rules for legislators (prohibiting other work) increase accountability.

truthcom.direction_note: A truth and reconciliation commission contributes to historical accountability.

agap_1.direction_note: Selection of the head of state in AG approval is counted as a positive accountability check.

agap_2.direction_note: Selection of the head of government in AG approval is counted as a positive accountability check.

agap_3.direction_note: Involvement of the cabinet in AG approval raises accountability.

agap_4.direction_note: First/only chamber participation in AG approval is a positive accountability factor.

agap_5.direction_note: Second chamber participation in AG approval is a positive accountability factor.

agap_6.direction_note: Judicial council/commission involvement in AG approval enhances accountability.

agap_7.direction_note: Judiciary involvement in AG approval is a positive accountability check.

agnom_1.direction_note: Head of state participation in AG nomination is scored positively for accountability.

agnom_2.direction_note: Head of government participation in AG nomination is scored positively for accountability.

agnom_3.direction_note: Cabinet participation in AG nomination is a positive accountability factor.

agnom_4.direction_note: First/only chamber participation in AG nomination is a positive accountability factor.

agnom_5.direction_note: Second chamber participation in AG nomination is a positive accountability factor.

agnom_6.direction_note: Judicial council/commission participation in AG nomination enhances accountability.

agnom_7.direction_note: Judiciary participation in AG nomination is a positive accountability factor.

agres_1.direction_note: Eligibility restrictions based on party affiliation reduce accountability, so selection scores low.

agres_5.direction_note: Citizenship‑only restriction is neutral to accountability; scoring reflects modest impact.

agres_6.direction_note: Native‑citizen restriction reduces inclusiveness and thus accountability.

agres_7.direction_note: Educational qualifications for AG reduce the pool and can lower accountability.

agres_8.direction_note: Non‑felon requirement is a standard integrity check; scoring treats it as neutral to accountability.

agres_9.direction_note: National‑status restriction limits eligibility and thus reduces accountability.

achighed.direction_note: Higher codes indicate greater access to higher education.

asylum.direction_note: Provision of asylum rights is positive for equality.

childwrk.direction_note: Limits on child employment protect equality.

civmar.direction_note: Civil marriage provision promotes secular equality.

colony.direction_note: Abandoning occupied territories is more egalitarian.

colrule.direction_note: Full citizenship for colonial populations is most egalitarian.

cultrght.direction_note: State duty to protect culture is positive for minority rights.

equal.direction_note: Explicit equality or non‑discrimination clause is maximally protective.

ethincl.direction_note: National integration provisions are positive for ethnic equality.

indcit.direction_note: Granting full citizenship to indigenous groups is highly egalitarian.

juvenile.direction_note: Special juvenile rights improve procedural equality.

lang.direction_note: More inclusive language regimes score higher.

langprot.direction_note: Constitutional protection of languages is positive.

lhquota.direction_note: Quota provision for groups in the first chamber is egalitarian.

marriage.direction_note: General right to marry is most inclusive; gender‑specific is less.

matequal.direction_note: Matrimonial equality is a positive equality provision.

nat.direction_note: Citizenship terminology affects political equality; citizens score highest.

natcit.direction_note: Provision for naturalization is positive for inclusion.

offrelw.direction_note: Any official religion limits religious equality.

opgroup.direction_note: Affirmative‑action provisions are positive for equality.

samesexm.direction_note: Recognition of same‑sex marriage is highly protective.

selfdet.direction_note: Self‑determination rights for groups are positive for collective equality.

trilang.direction_note: Right to a trial in a language understood promotes procedural equality.

uhquota.direction_note: Quota provision for groups in the second chamber is egalitarian.

voteres.direction_note: Absence of voting restrictions is most democratic.

voteun.direction_note: Explicit universal adult suffrage is maximally inclusive.

cabrestm_1.direction_note: Party‑based restriction for cabinet members is negative for equality.

cabrestm_2.direction_note: Gender restriction for cabinet members harms gender equality.

cabrestm_3.direction_note: Religious restriction for cabinet members harms religious equality.

cabrestm_4.direction_note: Ethnic restriction for cabinet members harms minority equality.

cabrestm_5.direction_note: Citizenship requirement for cabinet members is neutral.

cabrestm_6.direction_note: Native‑citizen requirement for cabinet members is restrictive.

cabrestm_7.direction_note: Education requirement for cabinet members is neutral.

cabrestm_8.direction_note: Non‑felon requirement for cabinet members is neutral.

cabrestm_9.direction_note: National requirement for cabinet members is neutral.

equalgr_1.direction_note: Protection of gender from discrimination is maximally positive.

equalgr_2.direction_note: Protection based on nationality is highly positive.

equalgr_3.direction_note: Protection based on country of origin is highly positive.

equalgr_4.direction_note: Protection based on race is highly positive.

equalgr_5.direction_note: Protection based on language is highly positive.

equalgr_6.direction_note: Protection based on religion is highly positive.

equalgr_7.direction_note: Protection based on sexual orientation is highly positive.

equalgr_9.direction_note: Protection of disabled persons is highly positive.

equalgr_10.direction_note: Protection based on color is highly positive.

equalgr_11.direction_note: Protection based on creed/beliefs is positive.

equalgr_12.direction_note: Protection based on social status is positive.

equalgr_13.direction_note: Protection of financial/property ownership is positive.

equalgr_14.direction_note: Protection of tribe/ clan is highly positive for indigenous groups.

equalgr_15.direction_note: Protection of political parties is positive for pluralism.

equalgr_16.direction_note: Protection based on parentage is positive.

federal_1.direction_note: Recognition of local governments is tangential to equality.

federal_2.direction_note: Recognition of subnational units is tangential to equality.

federal_3.direction_note: Recognition of autonomous indigenous groups is positive for minority rights.

finsup_1.direction_note: Constitutional support for the elderly is positive.

finsup_2.direction_note: Support for the unemployed is positive.

finsup_3.direction_note: Support for disabled persons is positive.

hogrest_1.direction_note: Party‑based restriction for head of government is negative for equality.

hogrest_2.direction_note: Gender restriction for head of government harms gender equality.

hogrest_3.direction_note: Religious restriction for head of government harms religious equality.

hogrest_4.direction_note: Ethnic restriction for head of government harms minority equality.

hogrest_5.direction_note: Citizenship requirement for head of government is neutral.

hogrest_6.direction_note: Native‑citizen requirement for head of government is restrictive.

hogrest_7.direction_note: Education requirement for head of government is neutral.

hogrest_8.direction_note: Non‑felon requirement for head of government is neutral.

hogrest_9.direction_note: Residency period requirement for head of government is neutral.

hogrest_10.direction_note: Marital‑nationality restriction for head of government is negative for equality.

hogrest_11.direction_note: National requirement for head of government is neutral.

hosrest_1.direction_note: Party‑based restriction for head of state is negative for equality.

hosrest_2.direction_note: Gender restriction for head of state harms gender equality.

hosrest_3.direction_note: Religious restriction for head of state harms religious equality.

hosrest_4.direction_note: Ethnic restriction for head of state harms minority equality.

hosrest_5.direction_note: Citizenship requirement for head of state is neutral.

hosrest_6.direction_note: Native‑citizen requirement for head of state is restrictive.

hosrest_7.direction_note: Education requirement for head of state is neutral.

hosrest_8.direction_note: Non‑felon requirement for head of state is neutral.

hosrest_9.direction_note: Residency period requirement for head of state is neutral.

hosrest_10.direction_note: Marital‑nationality restriction for head of state is negative for equality.

hosrest_11.direction_note: National requirement for head of state is neutral.

indpolgr_1.direction_note: Granting voting rights to indigenous groups is positive.

indpolgr_2.direction_note: Representation in central government for indigenous groups is positive.

indpolgr_3.direction_note: Right to establish political parties for indigenous groups is positive.

indpolgr_4.direction_note: Tax exemption for indigenous activities is a positive fiscal concession.

indpolgr_5.direction_note: Special legal allowances for indigenous groups are positive.

indpolgr_6.direction_note: Right to internal governance for indigenous groups is highly positive.

indpolgr_7.direction_note: Cultural rights for indigenous groups are highly positive.

lhquotad_1.direction_note: Quota for women in the first chamber promotes gender equality.

lhquotad_2.direction_note: Quota for ethnic groups in the first chamber promotes minority representation.

lhquotad_3.direction_note: Quota for parties in the first chamber promotes political pluralism.

lhquotad_4.direction_note: Quota for religions in the first chamber promotes religious inclusion.

lhquotad_5.direction_note: Quota for social class in the first chamber promotes socioeconomic balance.

lhrest_1.direction_note: Party‑based restriction for first‑chamber members is negative for equality.

lhrest_2.direction_note: Gender restriction for first‑chamber members harms gender equality.

lhrest_3.direction_note: Religious restriction for first‑chamber members harms religious equality.

lhrest_4.direction_note: Ethnic/language restriction for first‑chamber members harms minority equality.

lhrest_5.direction_note: Citizenship requirement for first‑chamber members is neutral.

lhrest_6.direction_note: Native‑citizen requirement for first‑chamber members is restrictive.

lhrest_7.direction_note: Education requirement for first‑chamber members is neutral.

lhrest_8.direction_note: Non‑felon requirement for first‑chamber members is neutral.

lhrest_9.direction_note: National requirement for first‑chamber members is neutral.

rightres_1.direction_note: Restriction of property owners' rights is negative for equality.

rightres_2.direction_note: Restrictions on minorities' rights are highly negative for equality.

rightres_3.direction_note: Restrictions on women's rights are highly negative for gender equality.

rightres_4.direction_note: Restrictions on non‑property owners' rights are negative for equality.

rightres_5.direction_note: Restrictions on peasants' rights are negative for socioeconomic equality.

rightres_6.direction_note: Restrictions on immigrants' rights are negative for inclusion.

rightres_7.direction_note: Restrictions on elderly individuals' rights are negative for age equality.

rightres_8.direction_note: Restrictions on disabled persons' rights are highly negative for disability equality.

uhquotad_1.direction_note: Quota for women in the second chamber promotes gender equality.

uhquotad_2.direction_note: Quota for ethnic groups in the second chamber promotes minority representation.

uhquotad_3.direction_note: Quota for parties in the second chamber promotes political pluralism.

uhquotad_4.direction_note: Quota for religions in the second chamber promotes religious inclusion.

uhquotad_5.direction_note: Quota for social class in the second chamber promotes socioeconomic balance.

uhrest_1.direction_note: Party‑based restriction for second‑chamber members is negative for equality.

uhrest_2.direction_note: Gender restriction for second‑chamber members harms gender equality.

uhrest_3.direction_note: Religious restriction for second‑chamber members harms religious equality.

uhrest_4.direction_note: Ethnic/language restriction for second‑chamber members harms minority equality.

uhrest_5.direction_note: Citizenship requirement for second‑chamber members is neutral.

uhrest_6.direction_note: Native‑citizen requirement for second‑chamber members is restrictive.

uhrest_7.direction_note: Education requirement for second‑chamber members is neutral.

uhrest_8.direction_note: Non‑felon requirement for second‑chamber members is neutral.

uhrest_9.direction_note: National requirement for second‑chamber members is neutral.

votelim_1.direction_note: Restriction that voters must not be incapacitated is negative for voting equality.

votelim_2.direction_note: Criminal‑record restriction on voting is negative for equality.

votelim_3.direction_note: Male‑only voting restriction is highly negative for gender equality.

votelim_4.direction_note: Ethnic/religious identity voting restriction is highly negative for minority equality.

votelim_5.direction_note: Citizenship requirement for voting is neutral (common).

votelim_6.direction_note: Literacy requirement for voting is negative for inclusive suffrage.

votelim_7.direction_note: Property/monetary requirement for voting is negative for economic equality.

votelim_8.direction_note: Language‑knowledge requirement for voting is negative for linguistic equality.

votelim_9.direction_note: Residency‑period requirement for voting is neutral to equality.

votelim_10.direction_note: Marital‑nationality restriction on voting is negative for equality.

votelim_11.direction_note: Registration requirement for voting is neutral (administrative).

votelim_12.direction_note: National requirement for voting is neutral (common).

votelim_13.direction_note: Marital status restriction on voting is negative for gender equality.

votelim_14.direction_note: Military‑service restriction on voting is negative for civic equality.

votelim_15.direction_note: Clergy restriction on voting is negative for religious equality.

arms.direction_note: 1=Yes (right to bear arms) scores 1.0, 2=No scores 0.0, 96=Other (permission required) scores 0.5.

cappun.direction_note: 1=Universally prohibited (most protective) scores 1.0, 2=Prohibited except war scores 0.75, 3=Allowed scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

corppun.direction_note: 1=Universally prohibited scores 1.0, 2=Prohibited except war scores 0.75, 3=Allowed scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

cruelty.direction_note: 1=Universally prohibited scores 1.0, 2=Prohibited except war scores 0.75, 3=Allowed scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

excrim.direction_note: 1=Specifically allows extradition scores 0.0, 2=Specifically prohibits scores 1.0, 3=Conditional scores 0.5, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

exprcomp.direction_note: 1=Fair/just scores 0.75, 2=Full market value scores 1.0, 3=Appropriate scores 0.75, 4=Adequate scores 0.5, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

fndfam.direction_note: 1=Yes (right to found a family) scores 1.0, 2=No scores 0.0.

jointrde.direction_note: 1=Yes (right to join trade unions) scores 1.0, 2=No scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

offrel.direction_note: 3=National religion forbidden (secular) scores 1.0, 2=Special treatment scores 0.75, 1=National religion specified scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

partprh.direction_note: 4=No prohibition scores 1.0, 3=Certain types prohibited scores 0.75, 2=Certain parties prohibited scores 0.5, 1=All parties prohibited scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

proprght.direction_note: 1=Yes (right to own property) scores 1.0, 2=No scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

prtyduty.direction_note: 1=Yes (duty to join a party) scores 0.0, 2=No scores 1.0.

rellawv.direction_note: 1=Yes (law contrary to religion void) scores 1.0, 2=No scores 0.0.

reltax.direction_note: 1=Yes (tax‑free status for religious orgs) scores 0.0, 2=No (denied) scores 1.0, 96=Other scores 0.5, 98=Not specified scores 0.5.

resenex.direction_note: 4=Neither entry nor exit restricted scores 1.0, 1 or 2 (single restriction) scores 0.5, 3=Both restricted scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

scifree.direction_note: 1=Yes (right to enjoy scientific progress) scores 1.0, 2=No scores 0.0.

seprel.direction_note: 1=Yes (separation of church and state) scores 1.0, 2=No scores 0.0, 96=Other scores 0.5.

slave.direction_note: 1=Universally prohibited scores 1.0, 2=Prohibited except war scores 0.75, 3=Prohibited with other exceptions scores 0.5, 4=Allowed scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

strike.direction_note: 1=Yes (right to strike) scores 1.0, 2=Yes with limitations scores 0.5, 3=No scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

torture.direction_note: 1=Universally prohibited scores 1.0, 2=Prohibited except war scores 0.75, 3=Prohibited for confessions scores 0.5, 4=Allowed scores 0.0, 90=Left to law scores 0.5.

artspec_2.direction_note: 1=Selected (protection of artistic work) scores 1.0, 0=Not selected scores 0.0.

artspec_3.direction_note: 1=Selected (state duty to promote art) scores 1.0, 0=Not selected scores 0.0.

citrev_1.direction_note: 1=Selected (revocation upon other citizenship) scores 0.0 (restrictive), 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

citrev_3.direction_note: 1=Selected (revocation upon government employment) scores 0.0, 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

envref_1.direction_note: 1=Selected (state duty to protect environment) scores 1.0, 0=Not selected scores 0.0.

envref_2.direction_note: 1=Selected (people's duty to protect) scores 1.0, 0=Not selected scores 0.0.

envref_3.direction_note: 1=Selected (right to enjoy environment) scores 1.0, 0=Not selected scores 0.0.

expcond_3.direction_note: 1=Selected (expropriation for national defense) scores 0.0, 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

expcond_4.direction_note: 1=Selected (expropriation for land/natural resource preservation) scores 0.0, 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

expcond_5.direction_note: 1=Selected (expropriation for resource exploitation) scores 0.0, 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

expcond_6.direction_note: 1=Selected (expropriation for land reform) scores 0.0, 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

explim_1.direction_note: 1=Selected (only certain property types) scores 0.5, 0=Not selected (no limit) scores 1.0.

explim_3.direction_note: 1=Selected (allowed without compensation in war/emergency) scores 0.0, 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

explim_4.direction_note: 1=Selected (only through legal process) scores 1.0, 0=Not selected scores 0.0.

rellaw_1.direction_note: 1=Selected (religious law default if no other) scores 0.0, 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

rellaw_2.direction_note: 1=Selected (religious law a source) scores 0.5, 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

rellaw_3.direction_note: 1=Selected (religious law supreme source) scores 0.0, 0=Not selected scores 1.0.

attend.direction_note: Higher mandatory attendance and penalties increase legislative autonomy.

cabrestl.direction_note: Prohibiting cabinet members from sitting in the legislature (code 2) enhances separation of powers and legislative autonomy.

commit.direction_note: Constitutional mention of committees strengthens legislative organization.

hogdec.direction_note: Head‑of‑Government decree power reduces legislative autonomy.

hogdecex.direction_note: Natural expiration of decrees (code 2) limits executive dominance.

hogdecim.direction_note: Decrees that require legislative approval (code 3) protect legislative autonomy.

hoglegr.direction_note: Prohibiting the Head of Government from being a legislator (code 2) enhances legislative independence.

hosdec.direction_note: Head‑of‑State decree power curtails legislative autonomy.

hosdecex.direction_note: Natural expiration of Head‑of‑State decrees (code 2) is more favorable to the legislature.

hosdecim.direction_note: Decrees that need legislative approval (code 3) protect legislative autonomy.

housenum.direction_note: Presence of at least one chamber is essential for legislative autonomy.

immunity.direction_note: Limited or no immunity (codes 2‑3) better balances accountability and autonomy.

legapp.direction_note: Legislation that does not require executive approval (code 5) maximizes legislative autonomy.

legappdf.direction_note: Automatic enactment when the approving body takes no action (code 1) favors legislative autonomy.

legapppt.direction_note: Line‑item veto (code 1) limits executive overreach more than an all‑bill veto.

legdiss.direction_note: Dissolution power vested in the legislature itself (code 5) is most protective of autonomy.

legisl.direction_note: A constitution that provides for a legislature (code 1) is the defining feature of legislative autonomy.

legsupr.direction_note: Supermajority requirement (code 1) makes law‑making harder, reducing autonomy.

lhcohort.direction_note: Cohort structure has limited impact on autonomy; both options are treated as neutral.

lhlegis.direction_note: First chamber’s power to legislate (code 1) is essential for autonomy.

lhterm.direction_note: Shorter terms increase accountability and legislative autonomy.

overpct.direction_note: Lower thresholds for overriding a veto (code 1) favor legislative autonomy.

override.direction_note: The ability to override a veto (code 1) is a strong autonomy safeguard.

overwho.direction_note: Involving the public (code 6) or both chambers jointly (code 5) gives the legislature more decisive power.

quorum.direction_note: A quorum requirement (code 1) is necessary for functional autonomy.

quorumw.direction_note: Lower quorum percentages are more favorable to legislative autonomy.

remleg.direction_note: Provision for removal of legislators (code 1) can protect autonomy if the process is legislative‑driven.

treat.direction_note: Mention of treaties is only marginally related to legislative autonomy.

uhlegis.direction_note: Second chamber’s power to legislate (code 1) strengthens bicameral autonomy.

uterm.direction_note: Shorter terms for second chamber members increase accountability.

cabappr_1.direction_note: Selection of the Head of State as approver reduces legislative autonomy (1 = selected).

cabappr_2.direction_note: Head of Government approval (code 1) limits legislative autonomy.

cabappr_5.direction_note: Both chambers required (code 1) further strengthens legislative role.

cabappr_6.direction_note: Appointment on advice of majority party/legislature (code 1) supports legislative influence.

cabappr_7.direction_note: Appointment based on election results (code 1) is neutral to autonomy; scored as 0.5.

cabdiss_1.direction_note: Head of State dismissal power (code 1) reduces legislative autonomy.

cabdiss_2.direction_note: Head of Government dismissal power (code 1) reduces legislative autonomy.

cabdiss_3.direction_note: Legislative chamber dismissal power (code 1) enhances autonomy.

cabdiss_4.direction_note: Second chamber dismissal power (code 1) enhances autonomy.

cabdiss_5.direction_note: Both chambers jointly dismissing (code 1) further strengthens legislative control.

cabdiss_6.direction_note: Both chambers required separately (code 1) also supports autonomy.

cabdiss_7.direction_note: Public recall (code 1) is a direct democratic check, positive for autonomy.

cabdiss_8.direction_note: Public prosecutor dismissal (code 1) is neutral/negative; scored as 0.0 when selected.

exsess_1.direction_note: Head of State convening extraordinary sessions (code 1) limits legislative self‑determination.

exsess_2.direction_note: Head of Government convening extraordinary sessions (code 1) also limits autonomy.

exsess_3.direction_note: Government/Cabinet convening extraordinary sessions (code 1) reduces legislative control.

exsess_4.direction_note: First/only chamber convening extraordinary sessions (code 1) enhances autonomy.

exsess_5.direction_note: Second chamber convening extraordinary sessions (code 1) also enhances autonomy.

exsess_6.direction_note: Both chambers required jointly (code 1) further strengthens legislative autonomy.

hogadiss_2.direction_note: First/only chamber can approve dismissal of HoG (code 1) boosts legislative autonomy.

hogadiss_3.direction_note: Second chamber approval (code 1) also boosts autonomy.

hogadiss_4.direction_note: Both chambers required (code 1) maximizes legislative role in dismissal.

hogadiss_5.direction_note: Public approval (code 1) is a direct democratic check, positive for autonomy.

hogadiss_6.direction_note: Public prosecutor approval (code 1) is neutral/negative; scored 0.0 when selected.

hogadiss_8.direction_note: Constitutional council/court approval (code 1) is neutral; scored 0.5.

hogpdiss_3.direction_note: First chamber proposal (code 1) enhances legislative autonomy.

hogpdiss_4.direction_note: Second chamber proposal (code 1) also enhances autonomy.

hogpdiss_5.direction_note: Both chambers required to propose (code 1) further strengthens legislative role.

hogpdiss_6.direction_note: Public recall proposal (code 1) is a direct democratic tool, positive for autonomy.

hogpdiss_7.direction_note: Public prosecutor proposal (code 1) is neutral/negative; scored 0.0 when selected.

hogpdiss_9.direction_note: Constitutional council/court proposal (code 1) is neutral; scored 0.5.

hosadiss_1.direction_note: Head of Government approval for HoS dismissal (code 1) reduces legislative autonomy.

hosadiss_2.direction_note: First chamber approval for HoS dismissal (code 1) enhances legislative autonomy.

hosadiss_3.direction_note: Second chamber approval (code 1) also enhances autonomy.

hosadiss_4.direction_note: Both chambers required (code 1) maximizes legislative role.

hosadiss_5.direction_note: Public approval (code 1) is a direct democratic check, positive for autonomy.

hosadiss_6.direction_note: Public prosecutor approval (code 1) is neutral/negative; scored 0.0 when selected.

hosadiss_8.direction_note: Constitutional council/court approval (code 1) is neutral; scored 0.5.

hosadiss_10.direction_note: No approval needed (code 1) is highly detrimental to legislative autonomy.

hospdiss_1.direction_note: Head of Government can propose HoS dismissal (code 1) reduces legislative influence.

hospdiss_2.direction_note: First chamber proposal (code 1) enhances legislative autonomy.

hospdiss_3.direction_note: Second chamber proposal (code 1) also enhances autonomy.

hospdiss_4.direction_note: Both chambers required (code 1) maximizes legislative role.

hospdiss_5.direction_note: Public recall proposal (code 1) is a direct democratic tool, positive for autonomy.

hospdiss_6.direction_note: Public prosecutor proposal (code 1) is neutral/negative; scored 0.0 when selected.

hospdiss_8.direction_note: Constitutional council/court proposal (code 1) is neutral; scored 0.5.

leg_in_1.direction_note: Head of State initiation (code 1) is executive‑centric, less favorable to legislative autonomy.

leg_in_2.direction_note: Head of Government initiation (code 1) also reduces legislative autonomy.

leg_in_4.direction_note: Political party initiation (code 1) can be democratic; scored 0.5.

leg_in_5.direction_note: Member of first chamber initiation (code 1) strengthens legislative autonomy.

leg_in_6.direction_note: Member of second chamber initiation (code 1) also strengthens autonomy.

leg_in_7.direction_note: Public initiation (code 1) is a direct democratic mechanism, positive for autonomy.

lhselect_1.direction_note: Appointment (code 1) reduces electoral accountability, lowering autonomy.

lhselect_2.direction_note: Election by elite group (code 1) is less democratic than popular election.

lhselect_3.direction_note: Election by citizens (code 1) maximizes democratic legitimacy.

rempro_1.direction_note: Public involvement in removal (code 1) is a direct democratic safeguard, positive for autonomy.

rempro_4.direction_note: First chamber involvement (code 1) enhances legislative autonomy.

rempro_5.direction_note: Second chamber involvement (code 1) also enhances autonomy.

rempro_6.direction_note: Both chambers required (code 1) further strengthens legislative control.

specleg_1.direction_note: Special process for organic law (code 1) can limit ordinary legislative power; scored 0.5.

specleg_2.direction_note: Special budget bill process (code 1) may constrain legislature; scored 0.5.

specleg_3.direction_note: Special tax bill process (code 1) may limit legislative autonomy; scored 0.5.

specleg_4.direction_note: Special finance bill process (code 1) may constrain legislature; scored 0.5.

specleg_5.direction_note: Special spending bill process (code 1) may limit legislative power; scored 0.5.

treatap_1.direction_note: Head of State approval of treaties (code 1) centralizes power, reducing legislative influence.

treatap_2.direction_note: Head of Government approval (code 1) also centralizes power.

treatap_4.direction_note: First chamber approval (code 1) enhances legislative participation in treaties.

treatap_5.direction_note: Second chamber approval (code 1) also enhances participation.

treatap_6.direction_note: Both chambers required (code 1) maximizes legislative involvement.

treatini_1.direction_note: Head of State initiation of treaties (code 1) centralizes power, reducing legislative autonomy.

treatini_2.direction_note: Head of Government initiation (code 1) also centralizes power.

treatini_4.direction_note: First chamber initiation (code 1) enhances legislative role.

treatini_5.direction_note: Second chamber initiation (code 1) also enhances role.

treatini_6.direction_note: Both chambers required (code 1) maximizes legislative initiation power.

uhselect_1.direction_note: Appointment to second chamber (code 1) reduces democratic legitimacy.

uhselect_2.direction_note: Election by elite group (code 1) is less democratic than popular election.

uhselect_3.direction_note: Election by citizens (code 1) maximizes democratic legitimacy.

camppubf.direction_note: Public financing of campaigns enhances competition, so presence (code 1) is scored higher.

compvote.direction_note: Compulsory voting limits freedom of choice, so code 1 is scored lower.

democ.direction_note: Reference to democracy signals a democratic orientation; presence (code 1) is positive.

demonum.direction_note: More mentions of ‘democracy’ indicate stronger democratic commitment; higher counts receive higher scores.

ecterm.direction_note: Longer judicial terms can enhance independence; higher years receive higher scores (up to 100 = life tenure).

ecterml.direction_note: Fewer term limits increase judicial independence; stricter limits (codes 1‑4) receive lower scores.

electfin.direction_note: Spending limits curb undue influence; presence (code 1) is positive.

hogelect.direction_note: Election by citizens (code 2) is most competitive; hereditary or appointed selections are less competitive.

hoselect.direction_note: Election by citizens (code 2) maximizes competition; hereditary or appointed selections are less competitive.

hoselsys.direction_note: Majority two‑round (code 6) and alternative vote (code 5) are more competitive than simple plurality (code 1).

initiat.direction_note: Allowing citizen initiatives expands competition; code 1 is positive.

lhtterm.direction_note: Longer terms can reduce turnover and competition; shorter terms (1‑3 years) receive higher scores.

part.direction_note: Recognition of parties (code 1) is essential for competition; absence (code 2) is undemocratic.

partprf.direction_note: Preference for parties (codes 1‑3) restricts competition; no preference (code 4) is most democratic.

referen.direction_note: Constitutional provision for referenda (code 1) expands direct competition; absence (code 2) limits it.

votemin.direction_note: Lower voting age expands the electorate; 18 (code 1) is most inclusive.

ecap_1.direction_note: Inclusion of the Head of State in judge approval (code 1) can politicize the court; absence (code 0) is more neutral.

ecap_2.direction_note: Head of Government involvement (code 1) may reduce judicial independence; scored lower.

ecap_3.direction_note: Cabinet involvement (code 1) is a restriction; absence (code 0) scores higher.

ecap_4.direction_note: Legislative chamber involvement (code 1) can politicize appointments; scored lower.

ecap_5.direction_note: Second chamber involvement (code 1) is a restriction; absence scores higher.

ecap_6.direction_note: Judicial council involvement (code 1) may enhance independence; scored higher.

ecap_7.direction_note: Judiciary involvement (code 1) can protect independence; scored higher.

ecnom_1.direction_note: Head of State nomination power (code 1) can politicize the court; scored lower.

ecnom_2.direction_note: Head of Government nomination (code 1) is a restriction; absence scores higher.

ecnom_3.direction_note: Cabinet nomination (code 1) reduces independence; scored lower.

ecnom_4.direction_note: First chamber nomination (code 1) is a restriction; absence scores higher.

ecnom_5.direction_note: Second chamber nomination (code 1) is a restriction; scored lower.

ecnom_6.direction_note: Judicial council nomination (code 1) can protect independence; scored higher.

ecnom_7.direction_note: Judiciary nomination (code 1) supports independence; scored higher.

ecres_1.direction_note: Party‑based eligibility (code 1) restricts competition; scored lower.

ecres_2.direction_note: Gender‑based eligibility (code 1) limits competition; scored lower.

ecres_3.direction_note: Religious‑based eligibility (code 1) restricts competition; scored lower.

ecres_4.direction_note: Ethnic‑based eligibility (code 1) limits competition; scored lower.

ecres_5.direction_note: Citizenship requirement (code 1) can be restrictive; scored lower.

ecres_6.direction_note: Native‑citizen requirement (code 1) is restrictive; scored lower.

ecres_7.direction_note: Education requirement (code 1) can limit competition; scored lower.

ecres_8.direction_note: Non‑felon requirement (code 1) restricts competition; scored lower.

ecres_9.direction_note: National‑status requirement (code 1) limits competition; scored lower.

initiatp_1.direction_note: Signature requirement (code 1) makes initiatives harder; presence scores lower.

initiatp_2.direction_note: Legislative sponsorship requirement (code 1) restricts initiatives; scored lower.

initiatp_3.direction_note: Party support requirement (code 1) restricts initiatives; scored lower.

partunco_1.direction_note: Executive power to ban parties (code 1) can curb competition; scored lower.

partunco_2.direction_note: Legislature power to ban parties (code 1) can curb competition; scored lower.

partunco_3.direction_note: Judicial power to ban parties (code 1) can protect competition if impartial; scored higher.

partunco_4.direction_note: Constitutional court power to ban parties (code 1) can protect competition; scored higher.

partunco_5.direction_note: Electoral commission power to ban parties (code 1) can protect competition; scored higher.

partunco_6.direction_note: Electoral court power to ban parties (code 1) can protect competition; scored higher.

referenp_1.direction_note: Head of State initiating referenda (code 1) centralizes power; scored lower.

referenp_2.direction_note: Head of Government initiating referenda (code 1) centralizes power; scored lower.

referenp_3.direction_note: First chamber initiating referenda (code 1) is more democratic than executive initiation; scored higher.

referenp_4.direction_note: Second chamber initiating referenda (code 1) is democratic; scored higher.

referenp_5.direction_note: Both chambers required (code 1) ensures broad consensus; scored higher.

referenp_6.direction_note: Government/Cabinet initiation (code 1) centralizes power; scored lower.

referenp_7.direction_note: Judiciary initiation (code 1) can protect competition; scored higher.

referenp_8.direction_note: Ombudsman initiation (code 1) adds an independent check; scored higher.

referenp_9.direction_note: Automatic trigger by legislative veto (code 1) enhances citizen control; scored higher.

adterm.direction_note: Longer maximum terms (including life tenure) increase judicial independence.

adtermn.direction_note: More permissive term‑limit regimes (higher code) enhance independence.

chalstag.direction_note: Pre‑promulgation review gives courts earlier power to block unconstitutional laws, thus higher independence.

chfterm.direction_note: Longer terms for the chief justice increase independence.

chftrmnm.direction_note: More permissive limits on chief‑justice terms raise independence.

concop.direction_note: Mention of constitutional‑court opinions improves transparency and independence.

conlim.direction_note: Fewer limits on constitutional‑court judges’ terms increase independence.

conrem.direction_note: Special removal procedures can protect independence if they are restrictive; here presence is taken as a modest positive.

conterm.direction_note: Longer maximum terms for constitutional‑court judges increase independence.

fedrev.direction_note: Allowing federal review of sub‑national legislation can curb regional overreach and support rule‑of‑law, modestly enhancing independence.

hocop.direction_note: Mention of highest‑ordinary‑court opinions improves transparency and independence.

jrem.direction_note: Presence of constitutional removal provisions is a modest positive for independence.

judind.direction_note: Explicit constitutional declaration of judicial independence is critical.

judprec.direction_note: Stare‑decisis (higher courts binding) supports a predictable, independent judiciary.

judretir.direction_note: Absence of mandatory retirement (code 4) gives the greatest tenure security; the more judges forced to retire, the lower the score.

judsal.direction_note: Constitutional protection of judicial salaries shields judges from political pressure.

ordterm.direction_note: Longer maximum terms for ordinary judges increase independence.

ordterml.direction_note: More permissive term‑limit regimes for ordinary judges raise independence.

supterm.direction_note: Longer maximum terms for the highest ordinary court increase independence.

suptermn.direction_note: More permissive limits on highest‑court judges’ terms raise independence.

treatrvw.direction_note: Allowing constitutional review of treaties strengthens judicial oversight of international commitments.

unconper.direction_note: Lower voting thresholds (plurality) make it easier for courts to strike down legislation, enhancing rights protection.

adap_6.direction_note: Judicial council involvement in approval is positive for independence.

adap_7.direction_note: Judiciary involvement in approval is modestly positive.

interp_7.direction_note: Both chambers required spreads power; moderate score.

cabinet.direction_note: Presence of a constitutionally defined cabinet adds institutional constraints on the executive.

execindp.direction_note: An explicit claim of executive independence signals insulation from checks.

headforn.direction_note: Concentration of foreign‑affairs authority in a single office reduces executive constraints.

hogdiss.direction_note: Provision for dismissing the head of government creates a check on executive power.

hogimm.direction_note: Absolute immunity shields the head of government; limited or no immunity increases accountability.

hogpard.direction_note: Pardon power concentrates unchecked authority in the executive.

hogterm.direction_note: Longer maximum terms reduce the frequency of executive accountability.

hogterml.direction_note: Stricter term‑limit rules increase constraints on the head of government.

hosdiss.direction_note: Dismissal provisions for the head of state create a check on executive authority.

hosimm.direction_note: Absolute immunity shields the head of state; limited or no immunity raises accountability.

hospard.direction_note: Pardon power concentrates unchecked authority in the head of state.

hosterm.direction_note: Longer maximum terms for the head of state reduce periodic accountability.

hosterml.direction_note: Stricter term‑limit rules increase constraints on the head of state.

cabappt_1.direction_note: Head of State nomination concentrates executive power.

cabappt_2.direction_note: Head of Government nomination concentrates executive power.

cabappt_3.direction_note: First chamber nomination introduces legislative oversight.

cabappt_4.direction_note: Second chamber nomination introduces legislative oversight.

cabappt_5.direction_note: Joint nomination by both chambers creates a strong legislative check.

hogdcond_1.direction_note: General dissatisfaction/no‑confidence as a ground increases executive accountability.

hogdcond_2.direction_note: Crimes/conduct grounds raise the threshold for removal, strengthening accountability.

hogdcond_3.direction_note: Treason as a ground provides a serious but limited basis for dismissal.

hogdcond_4.direction_note: Violations of the constitution as a ground increase constraints on the head of government.

hogdcond_5.direction_note: Incapacity as a ground adds a health‑related check on the head of government.

hosdcond_1.direction_note: General dissatisfaction/loss of confidence as a ground raises accountability of the head of state.

hosdcond_2.direction_note: Crimes/conduct grounds increase the ability to remove the head of state, strengthening constraints.

hosdcond_3.direction_note: Treason as a ground provides a serious but limited basis for dismissal.

hosdcond_4.direction_note: Violations of the constitution as a ground increase constraints on the head of state.

hosdcond_5.direction_note: Incapacity as a ground adds a health‑related check on the head of state.

access.direction_note: Accession provisions are only tangentially related to federalism.

fedsep.direction_note: Supremacy of subnational law indicates stronger federalism.

fedunit.direction_note: Federal or confederal descriptions signal higher federalism than unitary.

seccess.direction_note: Constitutional permission for secession reflects greater subnational autonomy.

resrce.direction_note: Reference to resource ownership can affect fiscal federalism but is not determinative.

amndappr_1.direction_note: Involving the head of state in amendment approval centralizes power, lowering federalism.

amndappr_2.direction_note: Involving the head of government centralizes amendment power.

amndappr_3.direction_note: Cabinet involvement in amendment approval centralizes authority.

amndappr_4.direction_note: Requiring only the first chamber can be centralist depending on chamber composition.

amndappr_5.direction_note: Requiring only the second chamber can be centralist.

amndappr_6.direction_note: Requiring both chambers may increase checks but does not directly empower subnational units.

amndappr_7.direction_note: Subsidiary‑unit approval of amendments strengthens federalism.

amndappr_8.direction_note: Public referenda add direct democratic input but are neutral for federalism.

amndprop_1.direction_note: Head‑of‑state proposal power centralizes amendment initiation.

amndprop_2.direction_note: Head‑of‑government proposal power centralizes amendment initiation.

amndprop_3.direction_note: Cabinet proposal power centralizes amendment initiation.

amndprop_4.direction_note: First‑chamber proposal power centralizes amendment initiation.

amndprop_5.direction_note: Second‑chamber proposal power centralizes amendment initiation.

amndprop_6.direction_note: Requiring both chambers to propose adds a check but does not empower subnational units.

amndprop_8.direction_note: Public initiative rights are democratically valuable but not directly federalist.

amndprop_9.direction_note: Judicial proposal power is neutral for federalism.

amndprop_10.direction_note: Ombudsman proposal power is neutral for federalism.

resrces_1.direction_note: Energy resource mention is only marginally related to federalism.

resrces_2.direction_note: Mineral resource mention is only marginally related to federalism.

resrces_3.direction_note: Farmland mention is only marginally related to federalism.

resrces_4.direction_note: Waterways mention is only marginally related to federalism.

resrces_5.direction_note: Forests mention is only marginally related to federalism.

resrces_6.direction_note: Fauna mention is only marginally related to federalism.

resrces_7.direction_note: Flora mention is only marginally related to federalism.

resrces_8.direction_note: Air/airways mention is only marginally related to federalism.

resrces_9.direction_note: General natural‑resource reference is only marginally related to federalism.

resrces_10.direction_note: Undeveloped land mention is only marginally related to federalism.

amend.direction_note: A higher score reflects the existence of a constitutional amendment procedure, indicating less rigidity.

amndamaj.direction_note: A higher score means amendments can be approved by a simple legislative majority, i.e., lower rigidity.

amndapct.direction_note: Higher percentages required for approval indicate greater rigidity, so scores decline with stricter super‑majorities.

unamend.direction_note: Presence of unamendable clauses makes the constitution more rigid, so a lower score reflects that.

conright.direction_note: Consumer protection provisions strengthen socioeconomic rights.

counscos.direction_note: State‑provided counsel enhances access to justice, a socioeconomic right.

econplan.direction_note: Mention of national economic plans is a borderline indicator of socioeconomic orientation.

edcomp.direction_note: Compulsory education is more protective when it exists.

edcompl.direction_note: Higher compulsory levels are more protective.

edfree.direction_note: Free education is a strong socioeconomic guarantee.

edfrel.direction_note: More extensive free‑education levels are more protective.

educate.direction_note: Any constitutional mention of education is a positive indicator.

env.direction_note: Environmental protection is only tangentially linked to socioeconomic rights.

forinves.direction_note: Protection or promotion of foreign investment is mildly supportive of socioeconomic rights.

freecomp.direction_note: A constitutional guarantee of a free/competitive market is a modest socioeconomic signal.

healthf.direction_note: Free government‑provided health care is a core socioeconomic right.

healthr.direction_note: A constitutional right to health care is a core socioeconomic guarantee.

leisure.direction_note: A right to rest and leisure is an important socioeconomic guarantee.

market.direction_note: Reference to a free market is a modest indicator of economic orientation.

provhlth.direction_note: State duty to provide health care is a core socioeconomic guarantee.

provwork.direction_note: State duty to provide work is a core socioeconomic guarantee.

remuner.direction_note: Right to just remuneration is a core socioeconomic protection.

safework.direction_note: A right to safe/healthy working conditions is a core socioeconomic right.

shelter.direction_note: The right to housing is a core socioeconomic guarantee.

socecon.direction_note: Explicit use of the term “socio‑economic rights” is a definitional indicator.

socialsm.direction_note: Mention of socialism is a weak proxy for socioeconomic commitment.

socsec.direction_note: Reference to social security of the nation signals a broad socioeconomic concern.

standliv.direction_note: A right to an adequate standard of living is a core socioeconomic guarantee.

tradeun.direction_note: A duty to join trade unions is generally negative for individual rights.

work.direction_note: A constitutional duty to work is negative for socioeconomic freedom.

envpart_1.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_2.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_3.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_4.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_5.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_6.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_7.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_8.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_9.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_10.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_11.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_12.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

envpart_13.direction_note: Specific environmental references are modestly related to socioeconomic rights.

couns.direction_note: Right to counsel is a core due‑process guarantee.

custlaw.direction_note: Reference to customary international law is only tangentially related to due process.

custlaw2.direction_note: Binding status of customary law modestly affects due‑process strength.

doubjep.direction_note: Prohibition of double jeopardy is a strong due‑process safeguard.

dueproc.direction_note: Explicit due‑process clause is definitional for the dimension.

examwit.direction_note: Right to examine evidence and confront witnesses is central to fair trial.

expost.direction_note: Prohibition of ex post facto laws is a core due‑process guarantee.

fairtri.direction_note: Explicit right to a fair trial is definitional for due process.

falseimp.direction_note: Right of redress for false imprisonment strengthens procedural safeguards.

grjury.direction_note: Grand jury involvement adds a layer of citizen oversight in indictments.

illadmin.direction_note: Protection against ultra‑vires administrative actions strengthens due process.

intlaw.direction_note: Reference to international law is only marginally related to due process.

judfin.direction_note: Finality of highest ordinary court decisions affects procedural finality.

jury.direction_note: Citizen participation in criminal trials enhances procedural fairness.

miranda.direction_note: Right to silence/self‑incrimination protection is a strong due‑process right.

presinoc.direction_note: Presumption of innocence is fundamental to fair criminal procedure.

rghtapp.direction_note: Explicit right to appeal strengthens procedural safeguards.

rulelaw.direction_note: General statement of rule of law is definitional for the dimension.

speedtri.direction_note: Right to a speedy trial is a core procedural guarantee.

treatst.direction_note: Treaty hierarchy affects due process only indirectly.

wolaw.direction_note: Explicit nulla poena sine lege clause is a core due‑process guarantee.
